serving.natural_language_portion: One standard fruit roll-up strip weighs approximately 14 g and provides around 39 kcal, 8.8 g carbohydrates, and 5.9 g sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Fruit Roll-Ups, strawberry flavour — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Branded Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 280 kcal, protein 1.9 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 63 g, sugar 42 g, sodium 0 mg, dietary fibre 10 g) are preserved from the input HPB Singapore record (confidence 0.95). USDA FoodData Central branded fruit roll-up entries (e.g., FDC ID 1100535) corroborate the energy and carbohydrate range. Saturated fat confirmed 0.0 g per USDA data for this product class. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, and micronutrient data not reported in HPB or USDA for this exact product and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fruit roll-up found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on analogous concentrated fruit confection / fruit leather entries in the Sydney GI database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The product is predominantly simple sugars (42 g/100 g) with minimal fat and moderate fibre (10 g/100 g); the high sugar density supports a high GI classification. Glycemic load per 14 g serving = (70 × 8.8 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 6.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sugar content (42 g/100 g) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain despite negligible sodium
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is 0 mg/100 g, the extremely high sugar load (42 g/100 g) exceeds the AHA 2018 threshold of 10 g/100 g for solid foods and promotes insulin resistance, sodium retention, and weight gain — all independent risk factors for elevated blood pressure; individuals with hypertension should avoid this product.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 42 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g ADA unsuitable threshold, causing rapid postprandial glucose spikes
type2_diabetes.guidance: With 42 g of sugar and 63 g of total carbohydrate per 100 g, and a high estimated GI of 70, this product causes rapid and pronounced postprandial glucose elevation; it is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and should be excluded from blood glucose management plans.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content (42 g/100 g) associated with dyslipidaemia, hepatic fat accumulation, and elevated triglycerides
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high free sugar content (42 g/100 g) in this ultra-processed snack is associated with elevated serum triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased hepatic lipogenesis — all established cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar at 42 g/100 g is strongly pro-inflammatory, upregulating TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high refined sugar content (42 g/100 g) promotes systemic inflammation by upregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-alpha and IL-6; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this product as it may exacerbate disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fruit Roll-Up is a flat, chewy, ultra-processed fruit-flavoured snack made from concentrated fruit puree, sugar, and corn syrup, typically sold in individually wrapped rolls of approximately 14 g. The product has a soft, pliable, and slightly sticky texture with an intensely sweet, fruity flavour derived primarily from added sugars and natural or artificial fruit flavourings rather than whole fruit. It is manufactured by concentrating and drying fruit puree blended with high levels of added sugar, then rolling the resulting sheet into a compact strip. Per 100 g, it provides 280 kcal, 1.9 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 63 g carbohydrate, 42 g sugar, 10 g dietary fibre, and 0 mg sodium. The fat content is confirmed zero by product formulation — this is a fat-free confection. The sodium content is confirmed zero per the HPB Singapore database. Despite the presence of 10 g dietary fibre per 100 g, the extremely high free sugar density (42 g/100 g) dominates the glycaemic response, resulting in an estimated high glycemic index of 70. Clinically, this product is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its concentrated sugar load. It is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Commonly consumed as a children's snack or lunchbox item in Western markets, particularly the United States.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in a fruit roll-up?
Is fruit roll-up suitable for hypertension?
What is the glycemic index of fruit roll-up?
How many calories in a fruit roll-up?

Micronutrient data not available in either HPB or USDA for this specific product.

Saturated_fat_g is 0.0 g — confirmed zero by product formulation. Sodium_mg is 0.0 mg — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore database. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 mg — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based product with no animal-derived ingredients. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific fruit roll-up product are not reported in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' from the input record has been removed as fruit roll-up is an ultra-processed concentrated fruit confection with no whole grain ingredients. The dietary_tag 'high_fibre' is retained as dietary fibre is 10 g/100 g. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on analogous concentrated fruit confection entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008.
